data.title: Sernova Biotherapeutics Taps Pericles Calias as Chief Development Officer, R&D Head
data.description:
Sernova Biotherapeutics said Monday that it appointed Pericles Calias as chief development officer and head of research and development.

The regenerative medicine company said Calias brings over two decades of global operational experience in drug product development.

"His deep expertise in leading all aspects of drug product and cell line development is invaluable to Sernova and will add momentum to advancing our Cell Pouch Bio-hybrid Organ as a much-needed functional cure for T1D in collaboration with Evotec," CEO Jonathan Rigby said.

Prior to joining Sernova, Calias held leadership roles at life sciences companies including Revolo Biotherapeutics, Cerecor, Sucampo Pharmaceuticals and Shire HGT, according to Sernova.

Sernova's shares closed 2.6% lower on Friday to $0.19 on the TSX.

data.title: S&P 500 index: Downside still beckons
data.description:
Key points:
  • U.S. equity index futures red; Nasdaq 100 down >1.5%
  • Euro STOXX 600 index off 0.8%
  • Dollar, bitcoin rise slightly; crude gains; gold slips
  • U.S. 10-Year Treasury yield falls to ~4.25%

S&P 500 INDEX: DOWNSIDE STILL BECKONS

The S&P 500 index ended Friday at 5,770.20, putting it down 6.09% from its February 19 record closing high. However, with e-mini S&P 500 futures suggesting a loss of about 80 points, or more than 1.3%, at Monday's open, the benchmark index appears poised for further downside pressure.

With this, the index looks like it will make another attempt to take out its rising 200-day moving average (DMA), which should be around 5,735:

Since reclaiming this closely followed long-term moving average on November 2, 2023, the SPX has not registered a daily close back below.

Therefore, bulls will be watching to see if the index can hold support in the 5,666.29-5,633.35 area and mount another recovery. This zone includes Friday's intraday low (5,666.29), the 23.6% Fibonacci retracement of the October 2023-February 2025 advance (5,665.13), the late-August high (5,651.62), the rising 233-day moving average, a Fibonacci-based long-term moving average (around 5,650 on Monday), and the 50% retracement of the August 2024-Febraury 2025 advance (5,633.35).

At the 50% retracement level, the SPX would be down 8.3% from its record closing high.

In the event of a deeper drop, the 61.8% retracement of the August-February advance is at 5,512.02. A decline to this level would put the S&P 500 down 10.3% from its record closing high.

Of note, in the July 2023-October 2023 selloff, which has been the most severe SPX decline since the 2022 bear market concluded, the index fell as much as 10.1% on a closing basis.

On a reversal back above the 200-DMA, initial resistance will be in the 5,773-5,783 area.

(Terence Gabriel)

data.title: Sector Update: Consumer Stocks Flat to Lower Premarket Monday
data.description:
Consumer stocks were flat to lower premarket Monday with the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund down 0.3% and the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund recently inactive.

Star Entertainment said it is assessing a recapitalization proposal from Bally's worth AU$250 million ($158.1 million), which could give the US-based company a controlling stake. Bally's shares rose past 9% pre-bell.

Hims & Hers Health's Apostrophe business said it's being discontinued, with all existing subscriptions to be canceled as of March 7. Shares of Hims & Hers Health were down over 3% premarket.

Wayfair shares declined by over 3% after the company said its Wayfair LLC unit plans to offer $700 million in senior secured notes due 2030 in a private offering.

data.title: Market Chatter: Namyang Dairy Returns to Profit, Targets KRW1 Trillion in Sales in 2025
data.description:
Namyang Dairy Products ended a six-year losing streak in 2024 and aims to surpass 1 trillion won in annual sales in 2025, a level that the company last touched in 2019, Pulse News reported Friday.

The company rebounded from the 2021 Bulgaris incident and management disputes by expanding product sales, exports, and business-to-business operations, to post 952.7 billion won in revenue and a net profit of 73.24 million won in 2024, the report said.

Analysts predict continued growth in 2025, with the company focusing on revamping its fermented dairy products to meet changing consumer preferences, it said.

Shares of Namyang Dairy Products fell nearly 2% at market close on Monday.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Spin Master Renews Global Master Toy Partner Agreement with Universal Products & Experiences
data.description:
Spin Master on Monday renewed its global master toy licensee agreement with Universal Products & Experiences for DreamWorks Animation's popular preschool brand Gabby's Dollhouse.

The company is preparing a new toy line which will launch in the fall, inspired by DreamWorks Animation's upcoming feature film, Gabby's Dollhouse: The Movie.

Shares of the company closed up $0.42 to $26.61 on Friday on the Toronto Stock Exchange.

data.title: Qunabox Group Swings to 2024 Loss
data.description:
Qunabox Group incurred an attributable loss of 1.67 billion yuan for the year 2024, compared with an attributable profit of 130.9 million yuan in the year-ago period, a Monday filing with the Hong Kong bourse said.

Loss per share was 8.4 yuan, whereas the company had made earnings per share of 0.56 yuan in 2023.

The marketing service company's revenue rose by 33% to 1.34 billion yuan in the year from 1.01 billion yuan a year prior.

The higher revenue was mainly due to a steady growth in China's economy, an expansion of service scope into new energy vehicles and household appliances, substantial investment in technological R&D and innovation, and the enhancement of the effectiveness of its AI interactive terminal network.

data.title: Beam Therapeutics Says Initial Data Show BEAM-302 Increases Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Protein
data.description:
Beam Therapeutics said Monday that initial data from a phase 1/2 trial of BEAM-302 show that a single dose led to "durable, dose-dependent" increases in total and functional alpha-1 antitrypsin, or AAT, protein across three initial dose levels, production of corrected protein M-AAT, and reduction in the amount of mutant protein Z-AAT in circulation.

BEAM-302 was well-tolerated at all dose levels, with an "acceptable" safety profile at all dose levels and no serious adverse events observed, the company also said.

AAT deficiency, or AATD, is a genetic disorder affecting the lungs and/or liver, leading to emphysema and liver disease.

Part A of the trial aimed to evaluate AATD in patients with lung disease, while part B will evaluate AATD patients with mild to moderate liver disease with or without lung disease, according to the company.

Beam said it intends to enroll and dose a fourth dose group in part A of the study and dose the first participant in part B in H2.

Shares of the company were down 2.6% in recent Monday premarket activity.

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0856 ET - The dollar weakens as markets grapple with recession fears. The ICE's DXY dollar index is on path for its lowest close since early November. The initial euphoria around President Trump's promised market-friendly tax cuts and deregulation gives way to concerns about tariffs possibly boosting inflation, keeping interest rates high and crippling economic growth. Declining Treasury yields, meanwhile, remove a key support for the greenback. The dollar weakens 0.7% versus the yen and around 0.1% versus the euro and the pound. It strengthens 0.3% against the Canadian dollar. U.S. inflation data is due later this week. February CPI is expected to slow a little, in a WSJ survey. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0852 ET - U.S. inflation data later in the week will be key for the dollar's performance after Federal Reserve Jerome Powell said the central bank was in no rush to cut interest rates, XTB's Kathleen Brooks says in a note. Powell played down U.S. growth concerns Friday and signalled the Fed needs see a weaker labor market and progress with disinflation before cutting rates again. The shelter index has proven a sticky point for core inflation and rents have been affected by Californian wildfires, Brooks says. "Overall, if there is no improvement in this index, then we could see the dollar stage a broad recovery later this week." The inflation data are due Wednesday. The DXY dollar index falls 0.1% to 103.689.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0837 ET - Euro high-yield cyclical credits could see improved performance as eurozone economic outlook lifts, BNP Paribas credit strategists say in a note. "A too-cold economy is the key risk to cyclicals, and that risk is receding in Europe," they say. Euro junk bond credit spreads are likely to tighten if the region's economic conditions improve, BNP Paribas says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0832 ET - Investors rush back to the safety of U.S. government debt, driving Treasury yields lower, amid fears of economic weakness. President Trump stops short of ruling out a recession, sounding less optimistic than some of his advisers. Former central banker Mark Carney wins the leadership of Canada's Liberal Party on promises to buck Trump's agenda. China's inflation is lower than expected. U.S. consumer and wholesale prices data are due this week. The 10-year yield is at 4.230%, giving up most of the gains recorded after Fed Chair Powell confirmed the central-bank wait-and-see stance on Friday. The two-year is at 3.933%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0821 ET - The Canadian dollar faces further falls in the coming months even after President Trump postponed 25% tariffs on many imports from Canada for a month, ING analysts say in a note. Even without a flat 25% tariff, Canadian goods are being selectively targeted by the U.S., while reciprocal tariffs are looming, they say. Canada should be "disproportionately affected" by tariffs due to its high export volumes to the U.S., they say. With the dollar likely to recover this summer, USD/CAD could trade above 1.45, they say. USD/CAD rises 0.2% to 1.4404. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0804 ET - The proposed increase in defense spending by European governments is likely to support growth and drive euro credit spreads tighter, BNP Paribas credit strategists say in a note. "In periods where the private sector is relatively more reliant on fiscal impulse than on the credit impulse, euro investment-grade spreads should trade with less 'credit risk'," they say. Eurozone sovereign bond supply is likely surpass corporate bond supply as governments plan to raise spending, BNP Paribas says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0752 ET - German industry in January posted the strongest monthly output increase since August, though December's production losses were only partially offset and the volatile auto industry was the key driver of overall output, HSBC's Anja Sabine Heimann says. Indeed, with fragile orders, where underlying demand has started to weaken again, and persistent tariff uncertainty, no sustainable recovery in production is in sight, she says in a note. However, by contrast, the medium-term outlook has improved through the proposals of the incoming governing coalition. For industry, the CDU and SPD say they intend to cut bureaucracy costs, reduce electricity taxes, build new gas-fired power plants and present more subsidies for energy-intensive industries, Heimann notes. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0734 ET - The U.S. is in a unique position when it comes to global arms exports, with a share of 43%, more than four times as much as France, the next largest exporter, says Mathew George, program director at the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute (SIPRI). "The USA continues to be the supplier of choice for advanced long-range strike capabilities like combat aircraft," George says. For the first time in two decades, the largest share of U.S. arms exports in 2020-24 went to Europe, at 35%, instead of the Middle East (33%), according to a SIPRI report published Monday. Saudi Arabia was the top single recipient of U.S. arms, representing 12% of U.S. arms exports, the report states. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0735 ET - Talks between Germany's likely coalition partners over the weekend suggest that a deal to form the next government could be less supportive of potential growth and more inflationary than last week's fiscal expansion suggested, says Oliver Rakau, chief Germany economist at Oxford Economics. "This may send yields higher as the bond market may anticipate more European Central Bank policy tightening," he writes in a note. Some measures address existing economic problems and should strengthen the supply side of the economy. But others look ineffective, driven by special interests or of a largely consumptive nature, Rakau says. While spending cuts are intended, there are risks that existing investment will be offloaded to the off-budget infrastructure fund to create space in the otherwise tight core budget, he adds. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0732 ET - Macroeconomic data is likely to be the main driver of U.S. and eurozone credit market movements, BNP Paribas credit strategists say in a note. "For now that data remains mid-range between too hot/too cold across both U.S. and Europe," the strategists say. European macro economic data looks favorable, making euro credit attractive, they add. "U.S. credit has corrected too much in our view, positioning has retraced and our tactical indicators are giving buy-signals," they say. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0731 ET - Shares in London-listed miners fell on Chinese deflation fears and the prospect of a recession in the U.S. In European late morning trade, Copper miner Antofagasta was the largest faller. It's shares traded down 3.6% at 1778.50 pence, while Anglo American fell 2.7% to 2317.50 pence. Silver and gold miner Fresnillo fell 1.5% to 836.00 pence, and Glencore fell 0.6% at 317.10 pence. Data showed that China's inflation rate was negative in February and domestic demand was weak, provoking concerns given the threat tariffs could pose to the country's exports, AJ Bell's Russ Mould writes. Separately, President Trump on Sunday refused to rule out a recession in the U.S. this year. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0731 ET - The U.S. supplied 64% of the arms imported by European members of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization between 2020 and 2024, according to a report by the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute published Monday. This compares with 52% supplied between 2015 and 2019. The other main suppliers for Europe were France and South Korea, accounting for 6.5% each, the report says. "The transatlantic arms-supply relationship has deep roots," Pieter Wezeman, senior researcher with the SIPRI Arms Transfers Program, says. "Imports from the USA have risen and European NATO states have almost 500 combat aircraft and many other weapons still on order from the USA." The U.S. increased its share of global arms exports to 43% between 2020 and 2024 from 35%, the report says. European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen on Sunday called on the 27 EU countries to buy more weapons from European manufacturers. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: Harvard Bioscience Inc expected to post earnings of 5 cents a share - Earnings Preview
data.description:
• Harvard Bioscience Inc is expected to show a fall in quarterly revenue when it reports results on March 12 for the period ending December 31 2024

  • The Holliston Massachusetts-based company is expected to report a 14.2% decrease in revenue to $24.15 million from $28.15 million a year ago, according to the mean estimate from 2 analysts, based on LSEG data.The company's guidance on November 7 2024, for the period ended December 31, was for revenue between $23.00 million and $26.00 million.

  • ​LSEG's mean analyst estimate for Harvard Bioscience Inc is for earnings of 5 cents per share.

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 2 "strong buy" or "buy," no "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell."

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts was unchanged in the last three months. ​

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Harvard Bioscience Inc is $6.25​, above​ its last closing price of $0.82. ​​​

The company's guidance on November 7 2024 for the period ended December 31 was for Earnings before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation and Amortization of between USD23 million and USD4.16 million.The company's guidance on November 7 2024 for the period ended December 31 was for gross profit margin between USD59% and USD60%.
